name: Eurycleia
shortDescription: Odysseus's loyal nurse who recognizes him by a scar and remains discreet about his return.
description: Eurycleia is a pivotal character in Homer's *Odyssey*, serving as the devoted nurse of Odysseus and later of his son, Telemachus. Purchased by Odysseus's father, Laertes, she remained a trusted member of the household, caring for Odysseus from infancy. Her significance lies in her unwavering loyalty and discretion. When Odysseus returns to Ithaca disguised as a beggar, Eurycleia is tasked with washing his feet. It is then that she recognizes a distinctive scar on his thigh, a mark he received during a boar hunt in his youth. Despite the shock of this revelation, Eurycleia maintains her composure and, upon Odysseus's command, keeps his identity a secret from the rest of the household, thereby playing a crucial role in Odysseus's eventual reclaiming of his throne and family. Her character embodies the virtues of faithfulness, prudence, and the enduring power of familial bonds. She is more than a servant; she is a confidante and a vital participant in Odysseus's heroic journey.
subtitle: Loyal Nurse and Keeper of Secrets
achievements: Alerting Odysseus to the disloyal maids
Remaining a steadfast member of Odysseus's household
quests: Aiding Odysseus in his return to power

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Eurycleia's origins are not explicitly detailed in the *Odyssey*, but she is presented as a slave purchased by Laertes, Odysseus's father. She is depicted as a figure of immense importance within the household, having nursed Odysseus since he was a baby. Her knowledge of Odysseus's past, particularly the scar he received during a boar hunt on Mount Parnassus, becomes crucial in the narrative. This scar, a physical manifestation of a formative event in Odysseus's youth, serves as the key to her recognition of him upon his return. The myth surrounding Eurycleia centers on her role as the keeper of secrets and the embodiment of unwavering loyalty, highlighting the significance of domestic bonds in the epic.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Eurycleia's role transcends that of a mere servant; she functions as a surrogate mother to Odysseus and Telemachus. Her presence underscores the importance of familial relationships and the enduring power of loyalty in a world often defined by treachery and deceit. She provides emotional support and practical assistance to both Odysseus and Telemachus, acting as a moral compass within the household. Her recognition of Odysseus is not merely a plot device but a testament to the enduring bond between them. By keeping his identity a secret, she actively participates in his plan to reclaim his kingdom, solidifying her significance as a key figure in the *Odyssey*.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Eurycleia symbolizes unwavering loyalty, domestic virtue, and the enduring power of memory. Her recognition of Odysseus through his scar represents the persistence of the past and the unbreakable bonds of family. The scar itself can be interpreted as a symbol of Odysseus's identity and his heroic past, which Eurycleia, as his nurse, is uniquely positioned to recognize. Her discretion in keeping Odysseus's secret highlights the importance of prudence and the ability to discern when silence is more powerful than speech. She is a representation of the unsung heroes who operate behind the scenes, providing essential support and guidance.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Eurycleia's character has resonated through the centuries as an archetype of the loyal servant and the devoted caregiver. She appears in numerous adaptations and retellings of the *Odyssey*, often portrayed as a figure of wisdom and strength. Her story serves as a reminder of the importance of loyalty, discretion, and the enduring power of familial bonds. In modern literature and film, characters inspired by Eurycleia often embody these same virtues, highlighting the lasting impact of her character on the cultural imagination. She is a testament to the enduring appeal of characters who embody quiet strength and unwavering devotion.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for the character of Eurycleia and her role in Odysseus's return to Ithaca.

sources.author: Emily Wilson
sources.description: A modern translation providing contemporary insights into the text and its characters.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: Eurycleia's defining characteristic and her unwavering devotion to Odysseus and Telemachus.

associations.name: Memory
associations.significance: Her memory of Odysseus's scar is crucial to his identification.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Ratnavali
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A devoted servant who plays a critical role in the hero's journey and remains loyal throughout trials.

companions.name: Odysseus
companions.description: The hero of the Odyssey, whom Eurycleia nursed and cared for.

companions.name: Telemachus
companions.description: Odysseus's son, whom Eurycleia also raised and protected.

cultural.modernLegacy: Eurycleia's character continues to be referenced in literature and art as a symbol of loyalty and domestic virtue. Her story highlights the importance of often overlooked figures in heroic narratives.
